questions.question: Why is it necessary for a parent process to call wait() after forking a child? (2-3 marks)
expectedPoints: To reap the child process and prevent it from becoming a zombie.
To obtain the exit status of the child process, allowing the parent to know how/why the child terminated.
To synchronize the execution of the parent with the completion of the child's task.

questions.question: Explain the concept of a zombie process. Describe how it is created and how it is destroyed. What is the role of the init process in this lifecycle? (5-10 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status has not been read by its parent.
Creation: A zombie is created when a child process terminates (via exit() or a signal) but its parent has not yet called wait() or waitpid() to read its status.
Destruction: The zombie is destroyed (reaped) when the parent finally calls wait() and retrieves the child's exit status, freeing its process table slot.
Orphaned Process: If a parent terminates before its child, the child becomes an orphan and is adopted by the init process (PID 1).
Role of init: The init process automatically calls wait() periodically to reap any orphaned children that have terminated, ensuring they do not become permanent zombies.
